frameworks.name: signal-detection theory
frameworks.one_line: perceptual choice plus confidence read out from MT direction-tuned population

frameworks.name: causal perturbation of sensory cortex
frameworks.one_line: focal optogenetic inactivation of direction-tuned MT neurons during decision

frameworks.name: downstream readout adaptation
frameworks.one_line: compensation in MT-to-decision readout over tens of minutes and sub-second windows

core_question: Does focal, reversible optogenetic suppression of direction-tuned MT neurons causally bias choice and confidence on a perceptual decision task in monkeys?
key_assumptions: MT neurons with shared direction tuning carry the evidence used for direction discrimination
choice and confidence can share a common evidence-readout mechanism
hyperpolarizing opsins provide a sufficiently focal and reversible inactivation
manipulations: randomly-interleaved trials with versus without MT inactivation
stimulus (and concurrent suppression) duration spanning below and above ~350 ms
exposure across the session to probe time-dependent compensation
key_findings: inactivating direction-tuned MT neurons systematically biased choice and confidence
behavioral effects attenuated over the course of each session
behavior was largely unaffected on trials longer than ~350 ms
choice and confidence showed parallel effects across conditions
interpretations: the downstream readout of MT compensates for perturbations on tens-of-minutes and sub-second timescales
parallel effects on choice and confidence are consistent with a common readout mechanism
limitations_noted: effects are transient, limiting the temporal window over which causal inferences hold
